flashcards.Back: Simple Network Management Protocol. An application layer protocol used for exchanging management information between network devices.
The centralized software system (NMS) used to monitor and manage network devices by sending requests and receiving notifications from agents.
A software component running on a network device (like a router or switch) that maintains its local management data and responds to manager queries.
A structured database maintained by the SNMP agent that defines all the objects (variables) a manager can monitor or configure on the device.
A unique numerical string used to identify a specific piece of information within the MIB hierarchy, such as CPU usage or interface status.
A message sent by the manager to the agent to retrieve the value of a specific OID from the device's MIB.
An SNMP request used to retrieve the value of the next OID in the MIB hierarchy, allowing the manager to 'walk' through a table of data.
An improvement introduced in SNMPv2c that allows the manager to retrieve a large block of data from the MIB in a single transaction.
A command sent by the manager to the agent to modify a configuration variable on the device, such as shutting down a port.
An unsolicited notification sent by an agent to the manager when a significant event occurs. it is an unreliable (UDP) 'fire-and-forget' message.
A notification similar to a trap, but it requires an acknowledgment from the manager. if no ACK is received, the agent retransmits the message for reliability.
The original version of the protocol. it uses simple community strings for security and lacks many of the performance features of later versions.
The most widely used version today. it introduces GetBulk and Inform messages but still relies on plain-text community strings for security.
The most secure version, providing robust authentication and encryption to ensure data confidentiality, integrity, and origin verification.
A simple plain-text password used in SNMPv1 and v2c to authorize communication between the manager and the agent.
A community string that only allows the manager to view (Get) device information but not make any changes (Set).
A community string that gives the manager authority to both view and modify configuration parameters on the device.
The standard destination port used by SNMP managers when sending requests to agents.
The standard destination port used by SNMP agents when sending Traps or Informs to the network management station.
The SNMPv3 security framework that provides authentication and encryption based on individual user accounts rather than shared passwords.
View-based Access Control Model. An SNMPv3 feature that allows administrators to restrict which parts of the MIB a particular user is allowed to see or change.
A unique identifier for every SNMPv3 entity (manager or agent) used to generate security keys and prevent replay attacks.
The hardware platform (server or workstation) that runs the SNMP manager software to monitor the entire network.
The lowest security level in SNMPv3, which uses username matching for authentication but provides no encryption or message integrity.
An SNMPv3 security level that provides message authentication (HMAC-MD5 or SHA) to verify the sender but does not encrypt the data.
The highest SNMPv3 security level, providing both MD5/SHA authentication and full DES/AES encryption for all management traffic.
The process where the manager periodically sends Get requests to agents to gather status data and build performance reports over time.
The hierarchical structure of the MIB database, starting from a root 'iso' and branching out to specific vendors like 'cisco'.
An individual data point in the MIB (like ifInOctets for interface traffic) that can be retrieved or modified by the manager.
A diagnostic procedure where a manager uses repeated GetNext requests to retrieve every OID value in a specific branch of the MIB tree.
